subject: English
chapter_title: Say the Right Thing (Wit and Humour)
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 1 periods = 40 minutes
Total: 1 periods · 0h 40min

periods.activity_title: Social Blunders: Introducing the Play and Scene I

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Choose statements that display polite behaviour with guests
Complete the Scene I speaker-words-reason table

phases.description: Teacher introduces the play by explaining that it is a two-scene comedy about a mother coaching her daughter for a guest visit. Students briefly discuss what 'Athithi Devo Bhava' means and why politeness with guests matters (page 70).

Students choose the statements from the list of five that display polite behaviour (page 70): offering water, enquiring about well-being, and accompanying guests to the door are polite; asking when they will leave and commenting critically are not.
Students read Scene I and complete the speaker-words-reason table (page 74), filling in Mary's reasons for two lines and Mrs. Shaw's words about the importance of kindness.

Teacher previews Scene II — and the comic catastrophe to come — building anticipation for the next period where Section C RFC work continues. Note: Section C coverage continues in next sessions with listening, speaking, writing, and grammar; remaining RFC and all other Section C spines will be addressed in teacher-led self-study due to the chapter running to the final period.

periods.teacher_notes: The prose_summary notes that Mrs. Shaw coaches Mary through several specific rules — be kind, say things that please people, and above all say 'Must you go?' not 'Can't you go?' — anchor the table task to these coaching instructions. This is the final LP period; the Section C RFC extract tasks, listening, speaking, writing, vocabulary-grammar, and beyond-text spines are not anchored but should be assigned as guided self-study.

section_contributions.implied_lo: Student identifies polite and impolite conversational behaviour in a dramatic dialogue.

tasks_anchored.task_brief: Choose statements that display polite behaviour with guests
Complete the Scene I speaker-words-reason table

items.source_lo: Student identifies polite and impolite conversational behaviour in a dramatic dialogue.
items.item_stem: In the play, Mrs. Shaw coaches Mary before the guests arrive. Based on the instructions Mrs. Shaw gives Mary in Scene I, which TWO of the following behaviours would Mrs. Shaw consider polite? Choose the correct options.

options.label: A
options.text: Telling a guest that their dog outside looks dirty.

options.label: B
options.text: Laughing and keeping the conversation flowing.

options.label: C
options.text: Asking guests to stay longer when they wish to leave.

options.label: D
options.text: Sharing your opinion about a neighbour's daily habits.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Students who choose this reveal they have confused what Mrs. Shaw forbids (personal remarks) with what a gracious host does; revisit Scene I's coaching dialogue.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Sharing opinions about neighbours is precisely the kind of conversational faux pas Mrs. Shaw warns against; a student choosing this may not have tracked the distinction between polite and impolite topics.

items.question_text: In the play, Mrs. Shaw coaches Mary before the guests arrive. Based on the instructions Mrs. Shaw gives Mary in Scene I, which TWO of the following behaviours would Mrs. Shaw consider polite? Choose the correct options.
